audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel chapter 24, first-pass restored draft
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for cave setting and bodily euphemism, numerical power imbalance, providential interpretation by David's men, robe cutting, conscience, restraint of followers, anointed-language misuse, self-lowering rhetoric, Saul's remorse, dynastic oath, boundaries, and non-reconciliation.

verses.restored: When Saul returned from after Philistines, they told him, saying, Look, David is in wilderness of En-gedi.
Saul took three thousand chosen men from all Israel and went to seek David and his men upon faces of Rocks of Wild Goats.
He came to sheepfolds by road, and a cave was there. Saul entered to cover his feet. David and his men were sitting in recesses of cave.

Men of David said to him, Look, this is day of which YHWH said to you, Look, I am giving your enemy into your hand, and you shall do to him as good in your eyes. David arose and secretly cut off edge of robe belonging to Saul.
Afterward David's heart struck him because he had cut off edge belonging to Saul.
He said to his men, Far be it from me by YHWH that I should do this thing to my lord, anointed of YHWH, to stretch out my hand against him, because he is anointed of YHWH.
David tore apart/restrained his men with these words and did not permit them to arise against Saul. Saul rose from cave and went on road.
Afterward David arose, went out from cave, and called after Saul, saying, My lord the king! Saul looked behind him, and David bowed with face to ground and prostrated himself.
David said to Saul, Why do you hear words of humans saying, Look, David seeks your harm?

Look, this day your eyes have seen that YHWH gave you today into my hand in cave. One said to kill you, but there was pity upon you, and I said, I will not stretch out my hand against my lord, because he is anointed of YHWH.

My father, see, yes, see edge of your robe in my hand. For in that I cut off edge of your robe and did not kill you, know and see there is no evil or rebellion in my hand. I have not sinned against you, though you hunt my life to take it.
May YHWH judge between me and you, and may YHWH avenge me from you, but my hand shall not be against you.
As proverb of ancients says, From wicked ones wickedness goes out; but my hand shall not be against you.
After whom has king of Israel gone out? After whom are you pursuing? After a dead dog, after one flea?
May YHWH be judge and judge between me and you. May he see and contend my case and judge me from your hand.
When David finished speaking these words to Saul, Saul said, Is this your voice, my son David? Saul lifted his voice and wept.
He said to David, You are more righteous than I, for you have repaid me good, while I have repaid you evil.
You have declared today how you dealt well with me, in that YHWH delivered me into your hand and you did not kill me.
For if a man finds his enemy, will he send him on good road? May YHWH repay you good for what you did to me this day.
Now look, I know that reigning, you shall reign, and kingdom of Israel shall be established in your hand.
Now swear to me by YHWH that you will not cut off my seed after me and will not destroy my name from house of my father.
David swore to Saul. Saul went to his house, and David and his men went up to stronghold.

translation_notes: Saul brings three thousand chosen men against a much smaller fugitive community. The power imbalance remains even when he is briefly vulnerable in the cave.
'Cover his feet' is a bodily euphemism for relieving himself. The scene should not be sexualized or used to shame ordinary bodily functions.
David's men interpret opportunity as Divine authorization. Group certainty and providential language do not make killing morally required.
Cutting the robe may carry symbolic royal meaning as well as proof of proximity. David's conscience responds even to a nonlethal violation.
David refuses to kill Saul and restrains his men. This is a significant rejection of assassination and retaliation, not proof that abused people must stay close to abusers.
'Anointed of YHWH' limits David's action in this scene but cannot establish royal impunity, immunity from removal, or a ban on lawful accountability.
David bows and calls Saul father/king while pleading his case. Survival deference under power should not be confused with free consent or endorsement.
David calls himself a dead dog and flea. These are self-lowering rhetorical images, not permission for dehumanizing language or low-self-worth spirituality.
Saul's tears, confession, blessing, and recognition may be sincere in the moment, but remorse without durable changed behavior does not automatically restore safety.
David does not return home with Saul; he returns to the stronghold. Forgiveness, mercy, or nonretaliation do not require reconciliation, trust, proximity, or removal of boundaries.
Saul's request about descendants reflects ancient dynastic violence. The oath does not erase later political complexity.
safety_note: Do not turn David's mercy into a command that survivors remain with violent people, surrender boundaries, or accept tears as proof of lasting change. Saul is still an armed king pursuing fugitives. 'Do not touch the anointed' cannot shield rulers, clergy, or charismatic leaders from investigation, removal, restraint, or justice. The chapter rejects assassination, but it does not demand passivity, reconciliation, or institutional impunity.
